name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ عَدُوِّى وَعَدُوَّكُمْ أَوْلِيَآءَ تُلْقُونَ إِلَيْهِم بِٱلْمَوَدَّةِ وَقَدْ كَفَرُوا۟ بِمَا جَآءَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْحَقِّ يُخْرِجُونَ ٱلرَّسُولَ وَإِيَّاكُمْ ۙ أَن تُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ رَبِّكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ خَرَجْتُمْ جِهَـٰدًا فِى سَبِيلِى وَٱبْتِغَآءَ مَرْضَاتِى ۚ تُسِرُّونَ إِلَيْهِم بِٱلْمَوَدَّةِ وَأَنَا۠ أَعْلَمُ بِمَآ أَخْفَيْتُمْ وَمَآ أَعْلَنتُمْ ۚ وَمَن يَفْعَلْهُ مِنكُمْ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ سَوَآءَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ (١)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Do not take My enemy and your enemy for friends, [secretly] offering them affection, if you have set out for jihad in My way and to seek My pleasure, for they have certainly denied whatever has come to you of the truth, expelling the Apostle and you, because you have faith in Allah, your Lord. You secretly nourish affection for them, while I know well whatever you hide and whatever you disclose, and whoever among you does that has certainly strayed from the right way. (1)
```

```arabic
إِن يَثْقَفُوكُمْ يَكُونُوا۟ لَكُمْ أَعْدَآءً وَيَبْسُطُوٓا۟ إِلَيْكُمْ أَيْدِيَهُمْ وَأَلْسِنَتَهُم بِٱلسُّوٓءِ وَوَدُّوا۟ لَوْ تَكْفُرُونَ (٢)
```

```english
If they were to confront you they would be your enemies, and would stretch out against you their hands and [unleash] their tongues with evil [intentions], and they are eager that you [too] should be faithless. (2)
```

```arabic
لَن تَنفَعَكُمْ أَرْحَامُكُمْ وَلَآ أَوْلَـٰدُكُمْ ۚ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَكُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (٣)
```

```english
Your relatives and children will not avail you on the Day of Resurrection: He will separate you [from one another], and Allah watches what you do. (3)
```

```arabic
قَدْ كَانَتْ لَكُمْ أُسْوَةٌ حَسَنَةٌ فِىٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَٱلَّذِينَ مَعَهُۥٓ إِذْ قَالُوا۟ لِقَوْمِهِمْ إِنَّا بُرَءَٰٓؤُا۟ مِنكُمْ وَمِمَّا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ كَفَرْنَا بِكُمْ وَبَدَا بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَكُمُ ٱلْعَدَٰوَةُ وَٱلْبَغْضَآءُ أَبَدًا حَتَّىٰ تُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَحْدَهُۥٓ إِلَّا قَوْلَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ لِأَبِيهِ لَأَسْتَغْفِرَنَّ لَكَ وَمَآ أَمْلِكُ لَكَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ ۖ رَّبَّنَا عَلَيْكَ تَوَكَّلْنَا وَإِلَيْكَ أَنَبْنَا وَإِلَيْكَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٤)
```

```english
There is certainly a good exemplar for you in Abraham and those who were with him, when they said to their own people, ‘Indeed we repudiate you and whatever you worship besides Allah. We disown you, and enmity and hate have appeared between you and us for ever, unless you come to have faith in Allah alone,’ apart from Abraham’s saying to his father, ‘I will surely plead forgiveness for you, though I cannot avail you anything against Allah.’ ‘Our Lord! In You do we put our trust, and to You do we turn penitently, and toward You is the destination. (4)
```

```arabic
رَبَّنَا لَا تَجْعَلْنَا فِتْنَةً لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَٱغْفِرْ لَنَا رَبَّنَآ ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٥)
```

```english
Our Lord! Do not make us a test for the faithless, and forgive us. Our Lord! Indeed You are the All-mighty, the All-wise.’ (5)
```

```arabic
لَقَدْ كَانَ لَكُمْ فِيهِمْ أُسْوَةٌ حَسَنَةٌ لِّمَن كَانَ يَرْجُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱلْيَوْمَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَ ۚ وَمَن يَتَوَلَّ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْغَنِىُّ ٱلْحَمِيدُ (٦)
```

```english
There is certainly a good exemplar for you in them—for those who look forward to Allah and the Last Day—and anyone who refuses to comply [should know that] indeed Allah is the All-sufficient, the All-laudable. (6)
```

```arabic
۞ عَسَى ٱللَّهُ أَن يَجْعَلَ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَ ٱلَّذِينَ عَادَيْتُم مِّنْهُم مَّوَدَّةً ۚ وَٱللَّهُ قَدِيرٌ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٧)
```

```english
It may be that Allah will bring about affection between you and those with whom you are at enmity, and Allah is all-powerful, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (7)
```

```arabic
لَّا يَنْهَىٰكُمُ ٱللَّهُ عَنِ ٱلَّذِينَ لَمْ يُقَـٰتِلُوكُمْ فِى ٱلدِّينِ وَلَمْ يُخْرِجُوكُم مِّن دِيَـٰرِكُمْ أَن تَبَرُّوهُمْ وَتُقْسِطُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُقْسِطِينَ (٨)
```

```english
Allah does not forbid you from dealing with kindness and justice with those [polytheists] who did not make war against you on account of religion and did not expel you from your homes. Indeed Allah loves the just. (8)
```

```arabic
إِنَّمَا يَنْهَىٰكُمُ ٱللَّهُ عَنِ ٱلَّذِينَ قَـٰتَلُوكُمْ فِى ٱلدِّينِ وَأَخْرَجُوكُم مِّن دِيَـٰرِكُمْ وَظَـٰهَرُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ إِخْرَاجِكُمْ أَن تَوَلَّوْهُمْ ۚ وَمَن يَتَوَلَّهُمْ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٩)
```

```english
Allah forbids you only in regard to those who made war against you on account of religion and expelled you from your homes and supported [the polytheists of Makkah] in your expulsion, that you make friends with them, and whoever makes friends with them—it is they who are the wrongdoers. (9)
```

## (Commentary)

The Surah mentions the alliance (موالاة) of the believers with the enemies of Allah among the disbelievers and their showing affection (موادة) toward them. It emphasizes the prohibition of this, opening and closing with it. It also contains some rulings concerning the emigrating women (المهاجرات) and the pledge of allegiance (بيعة) of the believing women. That it is a Medinan Surah is evident.

**His saying, the Exalted:** يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّخِذُوا عَدُوِّي وَعَدُوَّكُمْ أَوْلِياءَ تُلْقُونَ إِلَيْهِمْ بِالْمَوَدَّةِ **"O you who have faith! Do not take My enemy and your enemy for friends, offering them affection..."** (60:1) etc. The context of the verses indicates that some of the believers among the Emigrants (المهاجرين) used to secretly show affection to the polytheists (المشركين) in Mecca to protect those of their kin and children who remained there after they themselves had left by emigrating to Medina. Thus, the verses were revealed, and Allah forbade them from doing so. This is supported by what has been narrated that the verses were revealed concerning Hatib ibn Abi Balta'ah, who sent a secret letter to the polytheists in Mecca informing them of the intention of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to set out toward it for its conquest. He did this to have a "hand" (يدا) over them by which he might protect his kin and children in Mecca. Allah informed His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) of this, and the verse was revealed. His story will be presented to you in the following "Discussion on Traditions," Allah the Exalted willing.

So His saying: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّخِذُوا عَدُوِّي وَعَدُوَّكُمْ أَوْلِياءَ **"O you who have faith! Do not take My enemy and your enemy for friends"**—the enemy (العدو) is well-known and is applied to both the singular and the plural. What is intended in the verse is the plural, based on the evidence of His saying: أَوْلِياءَ **"friends"** and إِلَيْهِمْ **"to them"** and other such markers. They are the polytheists in Mecca. Their being His enemy is due to their taking partners for Him whom they worship instead of Allah, rejecting His call, and denying His Messenger. Their being enemies to the believers is because of their faith in Allah and their sacrificing their wealth and lives in His way; thus, whoever is an enemy to Allah is an enemy to them.

The mention of their enmity toward the believers—despite the sufficiency of mentioning their enmity toward Allah in the context of prohibition—is to emphasize the warning and prevention. It is as if it were said: "Whoever is an enemy to Allah is an enemy to you, so do not take him as a friend."

And His saying: تُلْقُونَ إِلَيْهِمْ بِالْمَوَدَّةِ **"offering them affection"**—affection (بالمودة) is the object of "offering" (تُلْقُونَ), and the preposition (الباء) is extra (زائدة), as in His saying: وَلا تُلْقُوا بِأَيْدِيكُمْ إِلَى التَّهْلُكَةِ **"and do not cast yourselves with your own hands into destruction"** (2:195). What is meant by "offering affection" is manifesting it or conveying it. The sentence is either an adjective (صفة) or a circumstantial clause (حال) for the subject of "do not take" (لا تَتَّخِذُوا).

And His saying: وَقَدْ كَفَرُوا بِما جاءَكُمْ مِنَ الْحَقِ **"while they have defied the truth that has come to you"**—it is the true religion (الدين الحق) which the Book of Allah describes and to which the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) calls. The sentence is a circumstantial clause.

And His saying: يُخْرِجُونَ الرَّسُولَ وَإِيَّاكُمْ أَنْ تُؤْمِنُوا بِاللهِ رَبِّكُمْ **"expelling the Apostle and you because you believe in Allah, your Lord"**—the sentence is a circumstantial clause. What is meant by expelling the Messenger and expelling them is their forcing the Messenger and the believers to leave Mecca and emigrate to Medina. And أَنْ تُؤْمِنُوا بِاللهِ رَبِّكُمْ **"because you believe in Allah, your Lord"** is connected to "expelling" with an implied preposition (اللام). The meaning is: they force the Messenger and you to emigrate from Mecca because of your faith in Allah, your Lord.

The description of Allah by His saying: رَبِّكُمْ **"your Lord"** is to point out that they are punishing them for a matter of truth that is obligatory, not a crime; for a person's faith in his Lord is an obligation upon him and is not a crime in any way.

And His saying: إِنْ كُنْتُمْ خَرَجْتُمْ جِهاداً فِي سَبِيلِي وَابْتِغاءَ مَرْضاتِي **"if you have set out to wage jihad in My way and to seek My pleasure"**—it is connected to His saying: لا تَتَّخِذُوا **"do not take"**, and the apodosis (جزاء) of the conditional is omitted, indicated by the connected phrase. Jihad (جِهاداً) is a verbal noun (مصدر) acting as an object of purpose (مفعول له), and seeking (ابْتِغاءَ) means searching for, and pleasure (المرضاة) is a verbal noun like satisfaction (الرضا). The meaning is: do not take My enemy and your enemy as friends if you have emigrated to struggle in My way and to seek My pleasure.

The restriction of the prohibition of their alliance and its conditioning upon their setting out for jihad and seeking His pleasure is of the category of conditioning a ruling upon a matter that is already established as occurring, to emphasize it and to signal the necessary connection between the condition and the ruling. It is like a father saying to his son: "If you are my son, do not do such-and-all."

And His saying: تُسِرُّونَ إِلَيْهِمْ بِالْمَوَدَّةِ وَأَنَا أَعْلَمُ بِما أَخْفَيْتُمْ وَما أَعْلَنْتُمْ **"You secretly proffer them affection, while I know best what you hide and what you disclose"**—"I kept a matter secret to him" (أسررت إليه حديثا) means I disclosed it to him in private. So the meaning of "you secretly proffer them affection" is that you inform them of the affection you keep secret—according to what al-Raghib said. Disclosure (الإعلان) is the opposite of concealment (الإخفاء). And "while I know best" (وَأَنَا أَعْلَمُ) etc., is a circumstantial clause for the subject of "you keep secret" (تُسِرُّونَ). "Know best" (أَعْلَمُ) is a superlative noun (اسم تفضيل), though some have suggested it could be the first-person singular present verb (فعل المضارع) made transitive by the preposition (الباء), because knowledge (العلم) is sometimes made transitive by it.

The sentence "you secretly proffer them..." etc., is an explanatory inception (استئناف بيانية), as if it were said after hearing the previous prohibition: "What have we done?" and the answer is: "You secretly inform them of your affection for them, while I know best what you have hidden and what you have manifested," meaning I know your speech and your action with a knowledge to which your concealment and manifestation are equal.

From this, it is known that His saying: بِما أَخْفَيْتُمْ وَما أَعْلَنْتُمْ **"what you hide and what you disclose"** together convey a single meaning, which is the equality of concealment and disclosure before Him, the Exalted, due to His encompassing of what is manifest and what is hidden. Thus, the objection that mentioning "what you hide" makes "what you disclose" redundant—since the Knower of what is hidden is a Knower of what is manifest by priority—is not applicable.

And His saying: وَمَنْ يَفْعَلْهُ مِنْكُمْ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ سَواءَ السَّبِيلِ **"and whoever of you does that has certainly strayed from the right way"**—the demonstrative "that" refers to the secret proffering of affection to them, which is the alliance (الموالاة). The "right way" (سَواءَ السَّبِيلِ) is the addition of an attribute to the described, meaning the even path and the straight path (الطريق المستقيم). It is the object of "strayed" (ضَلَ) or is in the accusative due to the removal of the preposition (بنزع الخافض), the estimation being "he has strayed *from* the right way." The way is the way of Allah the Exalted.

**His saying, the Exalted:** إِنْ يَثْقَفُوكُمْ يَكُونُوا لَكُمْ أَعْداءً **"If they confront you, they will be your enemies..."** (60:2). Al-Raghib said: *Thaqf* (الثقف)—with a *fatha* then a *sukun*—is skill in perceiving a thing and doing it. He said: It is said, "I *thaqaftu* such-and-such" when you perceive it with your sight due to skill in looking; then it is used metaphorically for perception even if skill is not present. (End quote). Others have interpreted it as "overcoming" (الظفر), perhaps aided by the suitability of the context; the two meanings are close.

The verse is intended to explain that secretly proffering affection to the polytheists will not benefit them at all in gaining their love or removing their enmity. Rather, the polytheists, despite the affection offered to them, if they were to encounter them and overcome them, would be their enemies without any change in the enmity within their hearts.

And His saying: وَيَبْسُطُوا إِلَيْكُمْ أَيْدِيَهُمْ وَأَلْسِنَتَهُمْ بِالسُّوءِ وَوَدُّوا لَوْ تَكْفُرُونَ **"and they will stretch out their hands and their tongues against you with evil, and they wish that you [too] should disbelieve"**—this is in the position of an explanatory conjunction (عطف التفسير) for His saying: يَكُونُوا لَكُمْ أَعْداءً **"they will be your enemies"**. Stretching out the hands with evil is a metaphor for killing, captivity, and other forms of torture. Stretching out the tongues with evil is a metaphor for reviling and insulting.

It appears that His saying: وَوَدُّوا لَوْ تَكْفُرُونَ **"and they wish that you should disbelieve"** is conjoined to the apodosis (الجزاء), and the past tense verb carries the meaning of the future as required by the conditional and its apodosis. The meaning is: they will stretch out their hands and tongues against you with evil, and they wish by that that you should disbelieve, just as they used to persecute the believers in Mecca and torture them, wishing thereby that they would apostatize from their religion. And Allah knows best.

**His saying, the Exalted:** لَنْ تَنْفَعَكُمْ أَرْحامُكُمْ وَلا أَوْلادُكُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ **"Your relatives and your children will not avail you on the Day of Resurrection..."** (60:3). This is a rebuttal to what might be imagined as an excuse for proffering affection to them—that in doing so there is protection for their relatives and children whom they left in Mecca among the polytheists from their harm.

The answer is that before you is a Day in which you will be recompensed for your disobedience and your evil deeds, among which is the alliance with the disbelievers. Your relatives and children—whose protection from the harm of the disbelievers you prioritized over the protection of yourselves from the punishment of Allah by abandoning the alliance with the disbelievers—will not benefit you today.

And His saying: يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَكُمْ **"He will decide between you"**—meaning Allah will separate between you on the Day of Resurrection by the severing of worldly ties, as He, the Exalted, said: فَإِذا نُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَلا أَنْسابَ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ **"When the Trumpet is blown, there will be no ties of lineage between them on that day"** (23:101). This is because kinship (القرابة)—which is the meeting of two or more humans in a single womb (رحم)—only exerts its effects of mercy, affection, intimacy, cooperation, support, partisanship, service, and other effects within the context of social life (الحياة الاجتماعية) to which man is driven by his natural need, according to the conventional views and beliefs (الآراء والعقائد الاعتبارية) that his social understanding has created within him. There is no trace of these views outside the context of social life.

When the realities (الحقائق) emerge, the veil is lifted, and the cover is removed on the Day of Resurrection, these views and assumptions will vanish from man, and the links of independence between causes and their effects will be severed, as He, the Exalted, said: لَقَدْ تَقَطَّعَ بَيْنَكُمْ وَضَلَّ عَنْكُمْ ما كُنْتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ **"Certainly [the ties] between you have been severed, and what you used to assert has forsaken you"** (6:94), and He said: وَرَأَوُا الْعَذابَ وَتَقَطَّعَتْ بِهِمُ الْأَسْبابُ **"and they see the punishment, and all their ties are cut"** (2:166).

On that Day, the bond of lineage will be severed, and no relative will benefit from his relative in any way. Therefore, it is not fitting for a person to betray Allah and His Messenger by allying with the enemies of the religion for the sake of his relatives and children, for they will not avail him against Allah on that Day.

It has been said: The meaning is that Allah will separate between you on the Day of Resurrection due to the terror therein which causes each of you to flee from the other, as expressed by His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ يَفِرُّ الْمَرْءُ مِنْ أَخِيهِ وَأُمِّهِ وَأَبِيهِ وَصاحِبَتِهِ وَبَنِيهِ لِكُلِّ امْرِئٍ مِنْهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ شَأْنٌ يُغْنِيهِ **"The day when a man will evade his brother, and his mother and his father, and his wife and his sons: every one of them will have enough to occupy him on that day"** (80:34-37). The previous view is more suitable to the context.

It has also been said: The meaning is that He will distinguish some of you from others on that Day, so the people of faith and obedience will enter Paradise, and the people of disbelief and disobedience will enter the Fire; and the believing relative in Paradise will not see his disbelieving relative in the Fire.

The critique of this is that while it is not objectionable in itself, it is not suitable for the context, as there is no indication in the context of the disbelief of their relatives and children.

It has also been said: What is meant by "deciding" (الفصل) is the decision of judgment (فصل القضاء), and the meaning is: Allah will judge between you on the Day of Resurrection.

The critique of this is the same as the previous one regarding lack of suitability to the case, for the decision of judgment is only suitable for disagreement (الاختلاف), as in His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فِيما كانُوا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ **"Indeed your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they used to differ"** (32:25), and there is no connection in the verse to that.

And His saying: وَاللهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ **"and Allah sees best what you do"** completes His saying: لَنْ تَنْفَعَكُمْ **"will not avail you"** as an emphasis for it. The meaning is: your relatives and children will not benefit you on the Day of Resurrection in removing the consequence of this betrayal and its like, and Allah sees best what you do; nothing of this betrayal is hidden from Him, so He will inevitably take you to account for it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قَدْ كانَتْ لَكُمْ أُسْوَةٌ حَسَنَةٌ فِي إِبْراهِيمَ وَالَّذِينَ مَعَهُ **"There is certainly a good exemplar for you in Abraham and those who were with him..."** (60:4) until the end of the two verses. The address is to the believers. An exemplar (الأسوة) is following and emulating (الاقتداء). In His saying: وَالَّذِينَ مَعَهُ **"and those who were with him"**, there is an apparent indication that there were those with him who believed in him other than his wife and Lot.

And His saying: إِذْ قالُوا لِقَوْمِهِمْ إِنَّا بُرَآؤُا مِنْكُمْ وَمِمَّا تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ **"when they said to their people, ‘Indeed we are done with you and with whatever you worship besides Allah’"**—meaning we are free of you and your idols—is an explanation of what the exemplification and emulation consist of.

And His saying: كَفَرْنا بِكُمْ وَبَدا بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَكُمُ الْعَداوَةُ وَالْبَغْضاءُ أَبَداً حَتَّى تُؤْمِنُوا بِاللهِ وَحْدَهُ **"We renounce you, and enmity and hatred have appeared between us and you for ever, until you believe in Allah alone"** is an explanation of the meaning of disavowal (البراءة) through its effect, which is renouncing them and being their enemy as long as they are polytheists, until they unify Allah, the Glorified.

What is meant by renouncing them (الكفر بهم) is renouncing their polytheism, as evidenced by His saying: حَتَّى تُؤْمِنُوا بِاللهِ وَحْدَهُ **"until you believe in Allah alone"**. Renouncing their polytheism is opposing them in it through action, just as enmity is separation and opposition in the heart.

Thus, they explained their disavowal of them through three matters: opposing their polytheism in action, enmity and hatred between them in the heart, and the continuation of that as long as they remain in their polytheism unless they believe in Allah alone.

And His saying: إِلَّا قَوْلَ إِبْراهِيمَ لِأَبِيهِ لَأَسْتَغْفِرَنَّ لَكَ وَما أَمْلِكُ لَكَ مِنَ اللهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ **"except Abraham’s saying to his father, ‘I will surely plead for forgiveness for you, though I cannot avail you anything against Allah’"** is an exception from what the preceding sentences indicate—that Abraham and those with him disavowed their polytheistic people in an absolute statement. They cut every bond that tied them to the people and connected them, except what Abraham said to his father: "I will surely plead for forgiveness for you" etc.

His saying: "I will surely plead for forgiveness for you" was not an act of alliance (توليا) from him, but rather a promise he made to him in the hope that he would repent from polytheism and believe in Allah alone, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَما كانَ اسْتِغْفارُ إِبْراهِيمَ لِأَبِيهِ إِلَّا عَنْ مَوْعِدَةٍ وَعَدها إِيَّاهُ فَلَمَّا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُ أَنَّهُ عَدُوٌّ لِلَّهِ تَبَرَّأَ مِنْهُ **"Abraham’s pleading for forgiveness for his father was only to fulfill a promise he had made him. So when it became manifest to him that he was an enemy of Allah, he disassociated himself from him"** (9:114). This implies that he (peace be upon him) only promised him because it had not yet become manifest to him that he was an enemy of Allah, rooted in his enmity and firm in his polytheism; so he was hoping he would return from his polytheism and desired that he would repent and believe. When his rooted enmity became manifest to him and he despaired of his faith, he disavowed him.

Furthermore, His saying, the Exalted, in the story of his disputation with his father in Surah Maryam: قالَ سَلامٌ عَلَيْكَ سَأَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكَ رَبِّي إِنَّهُ كانَ بِي حَفِيًّا وَأَعْتَزِلُكُمْ وَما تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ **"He said, ‘Peace be to you! I will plead with my Lord to forgive you. Indeed He is gracious to me. I will distance myself from you and what you invoke besides Allah’"** (19:47-48) includes his promise to his father of seeking forgiveness and his informing him of his withdrawal (الاعتزال). If his promise of seeking forgiveness were an act of alliance with his father, it would have been appropriate for him to say: "and I will withdraw from the people," not to say: "and I will withdraw from *you* [plural]," thereby including his father among those from whom he withdraws. And withdrawal is nothing other than disavowal (التبري).

So the exception is a connected exception (استثناء متصل) from the fact that they did not speak to their people except with disavowal. The resulting meaning is: they only offered them the speech of disavowal, except for Abraham's saying to his father: "I will surely plead for forgiveness for you," for it was neither disavowal nor alliance, but a promise he made to his father in the hope that he would believe in Allah.

There is a point here: the implication of the verse in *al-Tawbah*—"So when it became manifest to him that he was an enemy of Allah, he disassociated himself from him"—is that his decisive disavowal only occurred after the promise and after the manifestation of his enmity toward Allah. And His saying, the Exalted, in the verse we are currently discussing—"when they said to their people, ‘Indeed we are done with you’"—is an account of their decisive and final disavowal. Thus, what occurred in the exception regarding Abraham's saying to his father would be a promise occurring before his decisive disavowal, and of a different genus than the excepted-from (المستثنى منه); in that case, the exception would be disconnected (منقطعا), not connected.

On the premise of the exception being disconnected, it is possible that the exception is from His saying: "There is certainly a good exemplar for you in Abraham and those who were with him," insofar as it is restricted by His saying: "when they said to their people, ‘Indeed we are done with you’." The meaning would be: there is certainly a good emulation for you in the disavowal of Abraham and those with him from their people, except that Abraham said to his father such-and-such as a promise.

As for the premise of the exception being connected, the explanation is as previously mentioned. As for the view that the excepted-from is His saying: "There is certainly a good exemplar for you in Abraham," with the meaning: you have in Abraham an exemplar in all his traits except in his saying to his father: "I will surely plead for forgiveness for you," for there is no exemplification in that—

The critique of this is that His saying: "There is certainly a good exemplar for you in Abraham" etc., is not intended to make emulating Abraham (peace be upon him) obligatory in all his traits such that the promise of seeking forgiveness—or the seeking of forgiveness itself, which is one of his traits—would be excepted from them. Rather, it was only intended to make emulating him obligatory in his disavowal of his polytheistic people. The promise of seeking forgiveness in the hope of repentance and faith is not part of disavowal, even if it is not alliance either.

And His saying: وَما أَمْلِكُ لَكَ مِنَ اللهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ **"though I cannot avail you anything against Allah"** is the completion of Abraham's (peace be upon him) speech. It is an explanation of the reality of the matter: that his asking for forgiveness and seeking it from Allah is not the kind of seeking in which the seeker possesses from the one sought what he asks for. Rather, it is a request prompted by the poverty and lowliness of servitude (العبودية) before the richness and might of Lordship (الربوبية). It is for Him, the Exalted, to turn with His Noble Face and respond and have mercy, and it is for Him to turn away and withhold mercy, for no one possesses anything from Him, the Exalted, and He is the Owner of everything. He, the Exalted, said: قُلْ فَمَنْ يَمْلِكُ مِنَ اللهِ شَيْئاً **"Say, ‘Who can avail anything against Allah...’"** (5:17).

In short, His saying: "I cannot avail..." etc., is a type of admission of incapacity, as a corrective (استدراكا) to what might be sensed from his saying: "I will surely plead for forgiveness for you" of a trace of affirming power for himself. This is similar to the saying of Shu'ayb (peace be upon him): وَما تَوْفِيقِي إِلَّا بِاللهِ **"and my success lies only with Allah"** (11:88) as a corrective to what is sensed from his saying to his people: إِنْ أُرِيدُ إِلَّا الْإِصْلاحَ مَا اسْتَطَعْتُ **"I only desire to set things right as far as I can"** (11:88) of affirming strength and ability for himself essentially and independently.

And His saying: رَبَّنا عَلَيْكَ تَوَكَّلْنا وَإِلَيْكَ أَنَبْنا وَإِلَيْكَ الْمَصِيرُ **"Our Lord! In You do we trust, and to You do we turn, and toward You is the destination"** (60:4) etc., is from the completion of the speech narrated from Abraham and those with him, whom we are called to emulate. It is a prayer from them to their Lord and a supplication to Him following their harsh disavowal of their people, that He might protect them from its consequences and forgive them, so He does not disappoint them in their faith.

They opened their prayer with a preamble in which they mention their state regarding their disavowal of the enemies of Allah, saying: "Our Lord! In You do we trust, and to You do we turn," meaning: we were in a position in life where we could establish ourselves and manage our affairs. As for ourselves, we have turned (أَنَبْنا) and returned with them to You—which is turning (الإنابة). As for our affairs which we were to manage, we have left them to You and placed Your will in the place of our will; so You are our Trustee (وكيلنا) in them, managing them as You wish and how You wish—which is trust (التوكل).

Then they said: "and toward You is the destination," meaning that the destination of everything, whether an act or the doer of an act, is toward You. We have proceeded in our trust in You and our turning to You according to the reality of the matter—that the destination of everything is toward You—as we have emigrated with ourselves to You and left the management of our affairs to You.

And His saying: رَبَّنا لا تَجْعَلْنا فِتْنَةً لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَاغْفِرْ لَنا رَبَّنا **"Our Lord! Do not make us a [cause of] temptation for the faithless, and forgive us, our Lord"** (60:5) is the core of their prayer, asking Him, the Exalted, to protect them from the consequence of their disavowal of the disbelievers and to forgive them.

Temptation (الفتنة) is that by which one is tested. What is meant by making them a temptation for those who disbelieve is giving the disbelievers power over them to test them, so they bring out all the corruption in their power and harm them with various types of harm because they believed in Allah, rejected their gods, and disavowed them and what they worship.

They repeated His call—"Our Lord"—in their prayer time after time to stir the Divine Mercy.

And His saying: إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ **"Indeed You are the All-mighty, the All-wise"**—meaning the Victor who is never vanquished, precise in His acts; He is not unable to respond to their prayer and protect them from the plot of His enemies, and He knows by which way to protect.

The commentators have other various views in interpreting these two verses which we have overlooked for the sake of brevity; whoever desires them should consult the lengthy works.

**His saying, the Exalted:** لَقَدْ كانَ لَكُمْ فِيهِمْ أُسْوَةٌ حَسَنَةٌ لِمَنْ كانَ يَرْجُوا اللهَ وَالْيَوْمَ الْآخِرَ **"There is certainly a good exemplar for you in them—for those who look forward to Allah and the Last Day..."** (60:6) etc. The repetition of the discourse on the exemplar is to emphasize the obligation and to explain that this exemplar is for whoever hopes for Allah and the Last Day. Also, just as they are to be emulated in their disavowal of the disbelievers, they are likewise to be emulated in their prayer and supplication.

It appears that what is meant by hoping for Him, the Exalted, is hoping for His reward through faith in Him, and by hoping for the Last Day, hoping for what Allah has promised and prepared for the believers of reward; it is a metaphor for faith.

And His saying: وَمَنْ يَتَوَلَّ فَإِنَّ اللهَ هُوَ الْغَنِيُّ الْحَمِيدُ **"And whoever turns away [should know that] Allah is indeed the All-sufficient, the All-laudable"** is an expression of His, the Exalted, independence from their compliance with His command to disavow the disbelievers, and that they are the ones who benefit from that. Allah, the Glorified, is independent in His essence from them and from their obedience, and He is laudable (حميد) in what He commands and forbids them, as there is nothing in that except the rectification of their state and the happiness of their life.

**His saying, the Exalted:** عَسَى اللهُ أَنْ يَجْعَلَ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَ الَّذِينَ عادَيْتُمْ مِنْهُمْ مَوَدَّةً وَاللهُ قَدِيرٌ وَاللهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ **"It may be that Allah will create affection between you and those of them who are your enemies. Allah is all-powerful, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful"** (60:7). The pronoun "of them" (مِنْهُمْ) refers to the disbelievers whom they were commanded to treat as enemies, namely the disbelievers of Mecca. What is meant by creating affection between the believers and them is creating it by granting them success in [embracing] Islam, as occurred when Allah opened Mecca for them. It does not mean the abrogation of the ruling of enmity and disavowal.

The meaning is: it is hoped from Allah that He will create between you, O assembly of believers, and those of the disbelievers whom you have treated as enemies—the disbelievers of Mecca—affection by granting them success in Islam, so the enmity turns into affection. Allah is all-powerful, and Allah is all-forgiving of the sins of His servants, all-merciful toward them when they repent and submit. Thus, the believers should hope from Allah that He will exchange their enmity for affection through His power, forgiveness, and mercy.

**His saying, the Exalted:** لا يَنْهاكُمُ اللهُ عَنِ الَّذِينَ لَمْ يُقاتِلُوكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ وَلَمْ يُخْرِجُوكُمْ مِنْ دِيارِكُمْ أَنْ تَبَرُّوهُمْ وَتُقْسِطُوا إِلَيْهِمْ **"Allah does not forbid you in regard to those who did not make war against you on account of religion and did not expel you from your homes, that you deal with them with kindness and justice..."** (60:8) etc. In this verse and the one following it is a clarification of the prohibition mentioned at the beginning of the Surah. What is meant by those who did not fight the believers in religion and did not expel them are those other than the people of Mecca—those among the polytheists who did not fight them or expel them from their homes, such as the people of the treaty (أهل المعاهدة). Kindness (البر) is benevolence, and justice (الأقساط) is dealing with equity. And "that you deal with them with kindness" (أَنْ تَبَرُّوهُمْ) is a substitution (بدل) for "those who..." etc. His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُقْسِطِينَ **"Indeed Allah loves the just"** is the justification for His saying: "Allah does not forbid you..." etc.

The meaning is: Allah does not forbid you by His saying: "Do not take My enemy and your enemy for friends" from being benevolent and dealing with equity toward those who did not fight you in religion and did not expel you from your homes, because that is justice from you, and Allah loves the just.

It has been said: The verse is abrogated (منسوخة) by His saying: فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدْتُمُوهُمْ **"kill the polytheists wherever you find them"** (9:5). The critique of this is that the verse we are discussing, in its absolute sense, only includes the people of protection (أهل الذمة) and the people of the treaty; as for the people of war (أهل الحرب), it does not. The verse in *al-Tawbah* only includes the people of war among the polytheists, not the people of the treaty. So how can it abrogate that which does not conflict with it in indication?

**His saying, the Exalted:** إِنَّما يَنْهاكُمُ اللهُ عَنِ الَّذِينَ قاتَلُوكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ وَأَخْرَجُوكُمْ مِنْ دِيارِكُمْ وَظاهَرُوا عَلى إِخْراجِكُمْ أَنْ تَوَلَّوْهُمْ **"Allah only forbids you in regard to those who made war against you on account of religion and expelled you from your homes and supported [others] in your expulsion, that you make friends with them..."** (60:9) etc. What is meant by "those who made war against you" etc., are the polytheists of Mecca. Supporting the expulsion (المظاهرة على الإخراج) is aiding and assisting in it. And "that you make friends with them" (أَنْ تَوَلَّوْهُمْ) is a substitution for "those who made war against you" etc.

And His saying: وَمَنْ يَتَوَلَّهُمْ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ **"And whoever makes friends with them—it is they who are the wrongdoers"** is a restriction of isolation (قصر إفراد), meaning those who ally with the polytheists of Mecca and those who supported them against the Muslims are the wrongdoers who rebel against the prohibition, rather than those who ally with disbelievers in general; or it is an emphasis of the prohibition of allying with them.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّخِذُوا عَدُوِّي وَعَدُوَّكُمْ أَوْلِياءَ **"O you who have faith! Do not take My enemy and your enemy for friends"**—the verse: It was revealed concerning Hatib ibn Abi Balta'ah. The wording of the verse is general, but its meaning is specific. The reason for this was that Hatib ibn Abi Balta'ah had embraced Islam and emigrated to Medina, while his family was in Mecca. Quraysh feared that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) would raid them, so they went to Hatib's family and asked them to write to Hatib and ask him for news of Muhammad: does he intend to raid Mecca?

So they wrote to Hatib asking him about that, and Hatib wrote back to them that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) intended that. He gave the letter to a woman named Safiyyah, who placed it in her braids and set out. Then Gabriel descended to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and informed him of that.

The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) sent the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) and al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam in pursuit of her. They caught up with her, and the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) said to her: "Where is the letter?" She said: "I have nothing with me." They searched her but found nothing with her. Al-Zubayr said: "We do not see anything with her." The Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) said: "By Allah, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) did not lie to us, nor did the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) lie regarding Gabriel, nor did Gabriel lie regarding Allah, great is His praise. By Allah, you shall either produce the letter or I shall return your head to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)." She said: "Step away from me until I take it out." She then took the letter out from her braids. The Commander of the Faithful took it and brought it to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "O Hatib, what is this?" Hatib said: "By Allah, O Messenger of Allah, I have not turned hypocrite, nor have I changed or altered [my faith]. I bear witness that there is no god but Allah, and that you are truly the Messenger of Allah. However, my family and children wrote to me of the good treatment of Quraysh toward them, so I wished to repay Quraysh for their good social conduct." Then Allah revealed to His Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّخِذُوا عَدُوِّي وَعَدُوَّكُمْ أَوْلِياءَ **"O you who have faith! Do not take My enemy and your enemy for friends"**—until His saying—وَاللهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ **"and Allah sees best what you do"**.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ahmad, al-Humaydi, 'Abd ibn Humayd, al-Bukhari, Muslim, Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, Abu 'Awanah, Ibn Hibban, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, al-Bayhaqi, and Abu Nu'aym together in *al-Dala'il* narrated from 'Ali, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) sent me, al-Zubayr, and al-Miqdad, saying: "Go until you reach the Meadow (روضة) of Khakh, for there is a woman traveler (ظعينة) with a letter; take it from her and bring it to me."

We set out until we reached the Meadow, and there we found the woman traveler. We said: "Produce the letter." She said: "I have no letter with me." We said: "You shall either produce the letter or you shall take off your clothes." So she took it out from her hair-knots (عقاصها).

We brought it to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and it was from Hatib ibn Abi Balta'ah to some people of the polytheists in Mecca, informing them of some of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) affairs. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "What is this, O Hatib?" He said: "Do not be hasty with me, O Messenger of Allah. I was a man attached to Quraysh but was not of their own [kin]. Those of the Emigrants with you have relatives by whom they protect their families and wealth in Mecca. Since I lacked that lineage among them, I wished to do them a favor by which they might protect my relatives. I did not do that out of disbelief or apostasy from my religion." The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "He has spoken the truth."

'Umar said: "Let me, O Messenger of Allah, strike his neck." He said: "He witnessed Badr. And what do you know? Perhaps Allah looked upon the people of Badr and said: 'Do what you wish, for I have forgiven you.'" And there was revealed concerning him: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّخِذُوا عَدُوِّي وَعَدُوَّكُمْ أَوْلِياءَ تُلْقُونَ إِلَيْهِمْ بِالْمَوَدَّةِ **"O you who have faith! Do not take My enemy and your enemy for friends, offering them affection..."**

**I say:** This meaning is narrated in several reports from a number of companions such as Anas, Jabir, 'Umar, and Ibn 'Abbas, and a group of the Successors (التابعين) such as al-Hasan and others.

The narration, in terms of its content, is not without room for discussion:

Firstly: Because its apparent meaning—rather its explicit statement—is that Hatib ibn Abi Balta'ah deserved for what he did to be killed or given a punishment less than that, and that this was only averted from him because he was a participant of Badr (بدريا). Thus, a participant of Badr is not taken to account for the sin he commits, as explicitly stated by his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saying to 'Umar in this narration: "He witnessed Badr," and in the narration of al-Hasan: "They are the people of Badr, so avoid the people of Badr, they are the people of Badr, so avoid the people of Badr..." [repeated].

This is contradicted by what is in the story of the Slander (الإفك) that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), after the innocence of 'Aishah was revealed, carried out the prescribed punishment (حد) on Mistah ibn Uthathah, who was among the slanderers. This Mistah ibn Uthathah was among the foremost early Emigrants and among those who witnessed Badr, as in the *Sahihs* of al-Bukhari and Muslim, and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) punished him as stated by the many narrations regarding the interpretation of the verses of the Slander.

Secondly: Because what it contains of Allah's address to the people of Badr—"Do what you wish, for I have forgiven you"—which indicates that every sin they commit is forgiven for them, cannot be established, by necessity, except by the lifting of all religious obligations (التكاليف الدينية) from them, whether obligatory, prohibited, recommended, or disliked. There is no meaning to a masterly obligation (التكليف المولوي) being attached to a matter while the consequence of its violation is abolished and the act and omission are made equal for the subject, as indicated by his saying: "Do what you wish," given its self-evident appearance in general permissibility (الإباحة العامة).

The consequence of that would be:

First: The inclusion in forgiveness of those sins for which the intellect necessarily judges that pardon cannot include them without repentance, such as the worship of idols, rejecting Allah and His Messenger, denying the Prophet, fabricating against Allah and His Messenger, and mocking the religion and its necessarily established rulings. For the verses addressing them and forbidding them refuse the inclusion of forgiveness for them without repentance. Similar to these are the killing of a protected soul (النفس المحترمة) unjustly, corruption in the land, the destruction of crops and cattle, and the deeming permissible of blood, honor, and wealth.

It is well-known that the problem lies in the possibility of forgiveness being attached to such acts of disobedience and sins, not the actual attachment of it to them. Thus, it is not rebutted by saying that Allah, the Glorified, protects this forgiven subject from committing such acts of disobedience and sins even if He would have forgiven him had he committed them.

Second: That the saying "Do what you wish" would specify (يخصص) the generalities of all legal rulings (الأحكام الشرعية)—whether acts of worship or transactions—in terms of their subject matter, such that none of them would encompass the participants of Badr nor apply to them. If it were so, it would have been well-known among the companions and accepted by them that this group was liberated from every religious obligation and released from the bond of the duties of servitude. The participants of Badr themselves would have been most worthy of observing the meaning of this liberation among themselves given its importance. Yet there is no witness to testify to this in what is narrated of their reports and preserved of their traditions; rather, what is understood from their biographies—especially during the tribulations (الفتن) occurring after the passing of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—is the opposite of that, which no one can deny.

Furthermore, the liberation of a group of people of significant number and their release from the bond of obligation—allowing them to do what they wish and not care about disobeying Allah and His Messenger, no matter how great—contradicts the interest of the religious call (الدعوة الدينية) and the duty of commanding the right and forbidding the wrong (الأمر بالمعروف والنهي عن المنكر) and the spreading of the divine gnosis (المعارف الإلهية) brought by the Messenger through narration from him. For people would no longer have trust in them regarding what they say and narrate of the rulings of Allah and His Messenger, since there would be no harm upon them even if they brought every lie and fabrication or committed every vice and indecency, while the people know that of them.

This would also apply to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who is the master of the people of Badr, and Allah has sent him as a witness, a bearer of good news, a warner, a caller to Allah by His permission, and a luminous lamp. How can hearts find tranquility in the call of one for whom it is permissible to be clothed in every lie, fabrication, vice, and indecency? How can souls accept his being characterized by those noble attributes with which Allah praised him? Rather, how can it be permissible in His wisdom, the Exalted, to entrust the testimony and the call to one who is not secure in state or speech, and to call him a luminous lamp while He, the Exalted, has permitted him to revive falsehood just as he illuminates the truth, and has allowed him to misguide people when He sent him to guide them? The verses addressing the infallibility (عصمة) of the prophets and the preservation of revelation refuse all of that.

Moreover, that would corrupt the integrity of the address in many verses in which there is a rebuke of the companions and the believers for some of their failures, such as the verses revealed regarding the events of Uhud, al-Ahzab, Hunayn, and others, rebuking them for their defeat and fleeing from the battlefield, for which Allah has promised the Fire.

Among the clearest verses in this regard are the verses of the Slander, and among the people of the Slander was Mistah ibn Uthathah, the participant of Badr. In them is His saying, the Exalted: لِكُلِّ امْرِئٍ مِنْهُمْ مَا اكْتَسَبَ مِنَ الْإِثْمِ **"Every one of them shall bear what he has earned of the sin"** (24:11), and He did not exclude anyone from them. And His saying: وَهُوَ عِنْدَ اللهِ عَظِيمٌ **"while it is a monstrous thing in the sight of Allah"** (24:15), and His saying: يَعِظُكُمُ اللهُ أَنْ تَعُودُوا لِمِثْلِهِ أَبَداً إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ **"Allah advises you lest you should ever repeat the like of it, if you are believers"** (24:17).

Among the clearest verses whose meaning does not harmonize with the narration are these very verses for which the narration mentions the occasion of revelation: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّخِذُوا عَدُوِّي وَعَدُوَّكُمْ أَوْلِياءَ تُلْقُونَ إِلَيْهِمْ بِالْمَوَدَّةِ **"O you who have faith! Do not take My enemy and your enemy for friends, offering them affection..."**—the verses. In them is the like of His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ يَفْعَلْهُ مِنْكُمْ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ سَواءَ السَّبِيلِ **"and whoever of you does that has certainly strayed from the right way"** and His saying: وَمَنْ يَتَوَلَّهُمْ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ **"And whoever makes friends with them—it is they who are the wrongdoers"**.

It is well-known that the verses only directed the address and rebuke to the generality of those who believe, and they attribute the offering of affection and the secret affection for the disbelievers to the believers because one of them—namely Hatib ibn Abi Balta'ah—took the disbelievers as friends and betrayed Islam and the Muslims. Thus, the verses attributed the act of the individual to the whole and directed the rebuke and threat to everyone.

If Hatib—who was a participant of Badr, liberated, and for whom the pen was lifted—were addressed with the like of the saying: "Do what you wish, for I have forgiven you," such that there is no sin upon him in what he does, no straying in his regard, he is not characterized by wrongdoing, and no rebuke or threat attaches to him, then what is the reason for attributing the act of the individual—with its unpleasing attributes—to the whole, when no unpleasing attribute exists for the act of this individual according to the premise?

The matter would then lead to the assumption that an individual performs an act for which he is permitted, facing no rebuke or blame, yet the whole is rebuked and threatened for it. His speech, the Exalted, is far above the like of that.

In it [the same source]: al-Bukhari, Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Nahhas, and al-Bayhaqi in *Shu'ab al-Iman* narrated from Asma' bint Abi Bakr, who said: My mother came to me desiring [to visit] while she was a polytheist during the time of Quraysh—when they had made a treaty with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). I asked the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "Should I maintain ties with her?" Then Allah revealed: لا يَنْهاكُمُ اللهُ عَنِ الَّذِينَ لَمْ يُقاتِلُوكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ **"Allah does not forbid you in regard to those who did not make war against you on account of religion..."** So he said: "Yes, maintain ties with her."

In it: Abu Dawud in his *Tarikh* and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Qatadah: لا يَنْهاكُمُ اللهُ عَنِ الَّذِينَ لَمْ يُقاتِلُوكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ **"Allah does not forbid you in regard to those who did not make war against you on account of religion..."**—it was abrogated by: فَاقْتُلُوا الْمُشْرِكِينَ حَيْثُ وَجَدْتُمُوهُمْ **"kill the polytheists wherever you find them"**.

**I say:** You have already seen the discussion regarding this.

In *al-Kafi*: With its chain of narration from Sa'id al-A'raj from Abu 'Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "Among the firmest handles of faith (عرى الإيمان) is that you love for the sake of Allah, hate for the sake of Allah, give for the sake of Allah, and withhold for the sake of Allah, the Majestic and Exalted."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: With its chain of narration to Ishaq ibn 'Ammar from Abu 'Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "Everyone who does not love based on the religion and does not hate based on the religion has no religion."
